definition: Training builds a model (huge, batched, bandwidth-hungry — large GPU clusters). Inference runs it to serve requests (latency-sensitive, memory-capacity bound). The market is shifting spend toward inference at scale.
NVIDIA-popularized framing of a data center whose 'product' is tokens/intelligence: purpose-built, full-stack facilities that turn power and data into AI output at industrial scale.
The unit LLMs process (word-piece chunks). 'Tokens per second' and 'cost per million tokens' are becoming the throughput and economic KPIs for inference infrastructure.
Nations/enterprises building AI infrastructure within their own borders/control for data residency, security, and independence — a major driver of on-prem and regional buildouts.
Specialist providers (e.g., CoreWeave, Lambda) offering GPU capacity as a service, competing with hyperscalers — a fast-growing buyer segment for AI servers and racks.
Decoupling compute, memory, and storage so each scales independently and is composed on demand (enabled by CXL and fast fabrics) — improving utilization vs fixed servers.
Software-defined pooling of resources that are assembled into right-sized logical servers via API (e.g., HPE Synergy), then returned to the pool when done.
A programmable network processor (BlueField, Pensando) that offloads networking, storage, and security from the host CPU, freeing cores and enabling zero-trust isolation.
An industry community that open-sources data-center hardware designs (racks, power, cooling, NICs). Drives standardization for hyperscale and, increasingly, AI rack designs.
Protecting data while in use by running it in hardware-based Trusted Execution Environments (Intel TDX, AMD SEV-SNP), so even the host/hypervisor can't read it.
The full cost of infrastructure — acquisition plus power, cooling, space, software, and operations over its life. For AI, power/cooling and utilization increasingly dominate TCO.
Offloading heavy parallel work from CPUs to specialized processors (GPUs, DPUs, ASICs). The industry-wide shift underpinning AI and modern HPC economics.
